speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] with Sonia Berson.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Sonia, you were telling me that your husband passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He passed away in 1963. [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: The 8th of June. And then what happened? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I kept my business. My son and, and I ran the business. And about two years later, my friend of mine introduced me to Kal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got married when?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married-- let's see, the 9th of January in '66.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you a question, Sonia. Now that it's almost 50 years since World War II, is there any message that you'd like to give your son, your grandchildren, the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I like to give them a message is to be ever vigilant. I see things and I hear things that scare me. And I wish the world had learned a lesson, but from what things-- what's going on, I think that maybe they didn't learn from the past.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just wish that nobody-- my children, or my grandchildren, or whatever-- should ever have to go through what I went through. And I still think back, and I don't know how I did it, how I made it. What drove me?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- I think, that I for some reason, I thought that maybe my brother was alive. I thought that maybe my sister survived. Maybe somebody survived. And they're going to need me. And-- but many, many times, I felt like I cannot do it any longer, and I cannot do it anymore. And I could go to sleep and wish that I wouldn't wake up. And then I was happy that I woke up and to fight another day. And for some reason, in back of my mind, I knew that I'm going to survive. No matter what I saw around me, for some reason, I just had this-- that I have to survive, that maybe somebody survived that needs me. And I thought-- I don't know-- maybe that gave me strength and gave me-- to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Carol.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you like to introduce the rest of your family now, your husband? Stay there. We'll bring them in.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Carol, when do you see-- want to put the pictures in?

speaker: interviewer
text: After we finish this, we'll put the pictures in. Why don't you introduce your husband and your son to us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This Is my son, Morrie Symson and my husband, Kal Berson. And Kal, tell me what it's been like, very briefly, married to this lovely lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very happy. I was very happy to find her, because I was five years single. And just by luck, by luck between friends, where they know her, her friends, my friends, where they know me, and their friends was friends between them. So they, like, arrange it to see each other. And Morrie was the [NON-ENGLISH]. [LAUGHTER] Because after we was going around a couple of months, he says, Mom--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a couple months. Seven months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. If you love him, marry. And we was very happy for this. And he received me really very nice in the family. He was a big help, because I was really afraid-- a big son. And usually the kids, they not like it. And he was my friend. And I love it. And I'm happy still today. The only thing what I don't like it from Morrie is he got only two children, only two grandchildren. And I was dreaming for six. [LAUGHTER] But thank God. They are beautiful. They are good. They are loving. We love them. They love us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Now, Morrie--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me briefly what it was like growing up the child of survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father always worked very hard. And he felt, obviously, that he would have a long life to, you know, provide and, you know, provide for us and all that. And so, unfortunately, I think he worked probably too hard and then didn't realize that he made too many-- too many sacrifices prior to his getting sick. And he regretted it, you know, quite a bit when he was sick that he didn't really have a chance or didn't take the time, in a sense, to spend enough time, quality time. However, we did spend a lot of time together. I worked a lot in the store. We spent time, you know, as a family, you know, for all the holidays and-- but there's things that I think that we ended up doing without missed. But I think he expressed some of his regrets on his-- on his deathbed, that kind of made things, you know, decent, good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any values that you came away with from parents of survivors that you think maybe other children didn't get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I'm pretty vigilant about my thinking in reference to the maintenance of Judaism and loyalty to, to our people. I want my kids to, you know, of course, maintain the religion and, and, and raise their children Jewish and, you know, keep the family name, my family name, Symson, going since there's so few of us. [LAUGHS] That's basically it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Morrie was wonderful to me, when-- not to me, to his father-- when he was very sick. He came home from school. He was constantly at his bedside, both of us. He was-- after his father died, he was like my right hand. I couldn't have done what I did without him. He never gave me a hard time. He never-- he missed a lot. He didn't have a real good youth because his father got sick when he was maybe 14. And at that time, he already had to do a lot of things that-- other children had-- could go, and have fun, and do things. And he had to help me in the store and help his father. And after his father was very sick one time, they decided that UCLA that there's nothing they can do for my husband. And they wanted him to just to do exploratory surgery. And I had cousins in New York, and they were adamant not to do it, that I-- that they have better doctors in New York than UCLA. And I went to New York with my-- and I asked my husband if he wants to go. And he said yes. And we went to New York, and he was in the hospital for three weeks there. They came to the same conclusion that they want to do exploratory on, on him to find out what's wrong. So I said, if it comes down to this, I want to go home. And at that time, I left my store. Morrie was not even 16 years old. And at that-- this was at the time when there was the-- what-- the thing with Cuba. That when-- Kennedy wanted to invade Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bay of Pigs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I thought any minute, a war's going to broke out. And I'm in New York with my husband, and he's in the hospital there. And Morrie is here alone. And you know, and everything I had was, you know, in, in here and I was there. And my son was in, in Los Angeles. And we finally came home after three weeks. And my husband checked into UCLA. And they did exploratory. And they found out that he has cancer in the stomach that started not in the stomach, but the outside of the stomach. That's why they did all these x-rays and all-- he went, I don't know how many times to hospitals before. They couldn't find out what's wrong with him. And they saw. And they-- at that time, they told me that he has six months to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I begged the doctors not to tell him. And for a while, for a few days, they didn't tell him. Then he, he-- and I came into the room. I had to pretend everything's fine, you know? And you're going to go home, and things like this. So one day, he called-- you know, when the doctor was visiting him, he told him, please, tell me what's wrong with him-- with me. Tell me how long I have to live. I have a wife and a son that I have to think of. And, and, and, I-- and I want to know how long I have to live, because there's things that I have to take care of.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, the doctor told him that he has six months to live. And when I came that day to the hospital. It was UCLA. And I came in, you know, tell him everything, what's going on in the store and all these things, you know, pretending.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, Sonia, love, please don't do that anymore. I know what's going on. I, I forced the doctor to tell me. And I was so furious with him. But, he said, you know, Sonia, I had to do it, because, you know, how could I-- how could I-- if he asked me point blank? Anyway, after a while, he came home. He-- they started all kinds of experimental drugs on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for a while, he kind of recuperated a little. He even came with us to the store with me for a while. And he was determined that I learn the business. He was there with me, but he said, I'm not here. Just pretend I'm not here. Do the orders. Do this. Do that. Do everything, you know, whatever you have to do, just like I'm not here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and Morrie came every-- after work, he came to, to, to the store. And he was-- I couldn't have done without him. And when he was so very sick, he was with his father constantly, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end, I didn't even go to the store. I had just people taking care of the store. And we were with him 24 hours a day. And sometimes Morrie was furious with some people that came to see him, because they would tell him, oh, you're going to get better. You're going to be fine. And my husband also, when he knew that he's dying, he put all the bank books in my name. He put the liquor license in my name. He put everything in my name. After he died, I didn't have to do anything. And the store just went on. You know, I didn't have to run around. And I didn't have to-- he just prepared for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he saw to it that everything-- and, you know, I wanted to sell the store when he was so sick. I want to sell the store. I said, they are, you know, stealing me blind, because I wasn't-- you know, I wasn't there. I just went in the-- in the morning to maybe go to the bank or something like this. And when Morrie was in school, sometimes a friend of mine would have to come, because he wouldn't stay with the nurses. A friend of mine had to come to stay with him so I could go away. And many times, I thought this is-- you know, he's not going to, to live till next morning. I come the next morning, he was sitting in a chair, many times. He just-- his-- he died on a Saturday. On Wednesday, he asked the doctor if they found out anything new that they can still help him with. But he just-- you know, he was fighting for his life every hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had the will to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're very lucky that you have such a wonderful son and such a wonderful husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I would like to add to this, the conclusion is, for the last 25 years, because of the suffering, what she suffer and then all the time, for the last 25 years, we dedicated to work with charitable organizations and to help people with suffering, to help Israel, to help here. And our work for the last 25 years is just working for charities.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I have-- I have a wonderful son, wonderful grandchildren. Too bad my granddaughter doesn't want to be in it. And my grandson couldn't come. Otherwise, he would be here. My daughter-in-law has to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll show some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This must be a picture of the beginning of, let's see, '47, because Morrie must have been two, three months. He was born in November 11, so he-- he is a few months old here. So it must have been at the beginning of about February of '47. And that's my husband and me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not-- yeah. '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of the people in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time was Sonia Symson, Alter Symson, and Morrie Simpson. This picture was taken in the United States, approximately maybe '53, '54.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Mr. Symson, my former husband. This is Sonia Symson with, with my son. And it-- that must have been 1947, also in the beginning of 1947. Because my son is about six months old over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: [COUGHS] Your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's name is Morrie Symson. [COUGHS] This picture was taken about, let's see, about three, four years ago. And they honored my husband as Man of the Year in the Lodzer Organization. And the first person to my-- to my left is Morrie Symson, my son, Sandy Symson, my daughter-in-law, me, my granddaughter, Jennifer Hochman, my husband, Kal Berson, my step-daughter, Judy Hochman, and my-- Mr. Hochman, Alan Hochman, our husband-- our son-in-law. And on the bottom is Bryce, my granddaughter, and, and my grandson, Adam Symson, and Mrs. Hochman, my son-in-law's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at Friars Club.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about four or five years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1989, Probably. This is my grandson, Adam Symson. And this picture was taken in Los Angeles in 1987. And he was 13 years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: His bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was his bar mitzvah picture. This is my granddaughter, Bryce. And this is a school picture taken in 1993 in Los Angeles. This is my granddaughter, Jennifer Hochman. And she was 16 years old in this picture. And I have later pictures from her, but I think this is the nicest, so I want to show this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Los Angeles. This is a picture of myself and my husband, about 1999.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's at a party. And it was a beautiful, happy occasion for us to celebrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your husband and the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Berson and Kal Berson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
